raw_consumer_statement:
I am filing this complaint against Citibank , N.A . for failing to conduct a " reasonable investigation '' of a billing dispute ( Case ID : XXXX ) as mandated by the Fair Credit Billing Act ( FCBA ). 
On XX/XX/XXXX, I returned a rental vehicle to XXXX ( XXXX, XXXX ) at XXXX XXXX  to the short-term parking lot, per their instructions ( XXXX email attached ). The merchant charged me a {$3500.00} " full excess '' for damage they claim was found at XXXX XXXX, over three hours after I surrendered custody. I provided Citibank with irrefutable proof ( flight records, email stating where to return car ) showing I was inside a secure terminal and physically unable to have caused the damage during that window. 
Furthermore, I provided Citibank with evidence of fraudulent invoicing and bad faith documentation by the merchant : The merchant provided three conflicting financial demands for the same claim : XXXX, Pg XXXX ( XXXX ), XXXX, Pg XXXX ( XXXX ), and XXXX pg XXXX ( a non-itemized demand for " XXXX '' ). 

The car was not repaired for 6 weeks, and only a tire was repaired. 

Despite this, the merchant charged the full {$3500.00} ( excess ), with no evidence of repair. 
The merchant failed to provide a valid VAT invoice, a legal requirement for repair charges in XXXX, they also failed to show time-stamped photos with the Damage Evaluation Tool , per their policy, for the scratches/ scuffs. The merchant has 2 separate claims for the same incident. 
Citibank ignored these 10 documented material discrepancies ( attached Formal dispute summary ) and closed the dispute based solely on the merchant 's reports. I am requesting that the CFPB compel Citibank to reopen this investigation and address why they are permitting a merchant to charge over 10x their own documented repair estimate in violation of XXXX Merchant Rules .
